messages.content:
Note: This is in an alternate universe, This shop, despite being awesome, never existed, It does in this alternate universe, Also sorry for constantly showing you this, I’m just trying to make sure its perfect

We’ve loved being in this spot as part of the wonderful Glasgow community

Unfortunately, This shop is now closed, The realities of retail in the shopping landscape has made this decision unavoidable, We want to take the opportunity to thank you for shopping with us for so many years, You can find all about KikoRiki and its related online MMO RolyPolyLand at riki.com

Goodbye forever…

This sign was posted to the doors of Riki, a children’s shop in Glasgow on Wednesday

The image ends with a poignant “Goodbye forever…” above a image of a crying Krash the Rabbit

The store closed on Wedensday after 12 years trading at Sauchiehall Street, Glasgow

The shop sold merchandise related to the animated series KikoRiki, The cartoon is a Russian cartoon about circular colorful animals and their fun adventures, The Riki shop opened in 1995 was originally Golden Adventures and was a general toy shop akin to Toys R Us but in 2003 became Riki and became focused on the then new surge in KikoRiki popularity in Scotland, However in June 2007, the store closed for the last time, Riki Group’s reasoning was the “realities of retail” was unavoidable, The shop was also closed so Riki could focus on business in it’s native Russia and develop more exciting new animated series or movies


Hidden Glasgow - Goodbye Riki

Joeye posted on 23 June 2007
It’s the end of an era, Even bigger than the end of Evangelion, or even Christopher Eccleston leaving Doctor Who, The famous Riki store has gone, After about 12 years of selling toys and 4 years of its golden age of KikoRiki merchandise the store has unfortunately vanished, in its place is a rather upsetting image of Krash crying, It pains my heart to see the poor guy like this, Apparently “the realities of retail in the shopping landscape have made this change unavoidable” Whats most striking is “Goodbye forever…” written in a melancholic font, and the most striking part is that it’s showing Krash crying. My kids will miss the shop dearly. There is another sign giving the channel numbers for CITV, the channel that broadcasts the KikoRiki cartoon, Although there is an error, Telewest doesn’t exist anymore, it’s Virgin Media now. Anyways I was there on the last day, It all felt a bit more downbeat than usual, During the final day, the characters were giving reassuring smiles, reassuring us that we will still see them around Glasgow, and on CITV, The final half hour of the store had a looping pre-recorded announcement playing over the tannoy, sincerely thanking us for shopping over the years, “We would like to thank you who started shopping with us when we started as Golden Adventures, And especially when we were Riki” They also advertise CITV and RolyPolyLand “Catch us on the CITV channel everyday, and play our new online MMO RolyPolyLand” The message was very heartfelt, When I left at closing time they were already putting up posters, and the loop was replaced with a final announcement saying “Goodbye now, Take care, wherever you are” Then was there a sombre piece of music playing, before it fell silent as the lights were turned off,

Johnstone posted on 23 June 2007
It’s a very sad state of affairs that Riki has ceased to exist. A good shop, Now we will have to trek to Toys R Us, And that’s literally at the Fort Shopping Centre near the Barrowland Ballroom.

Harry replied on 23 June 2007
They should have kept it open, I am a fan of that show, Hopefully they find something good to take over the space, It will most likely be another Greggs, because it’s not like we’ve had enough of THOSE, right?

Chingling replied on 24 June 2007
I went past Sauchiehall Street today, I noticed M&S has a sign saying “Our dearest condolences to staff of Riki”, I also saw the Riki closure notice, How sad it all is, Here is an image “ByeRiki.png” Someone cheer poor Krash up, he’s had a rough day, I want to hug the little furball, Incidentally Toys R Us in St Enoch centre are now selling toys of the show, so maybe Riki worked something out with Toys R Us, I also contacted Riki and was told the Goodbye forever note was written by David Lewis, who founded the Golden Adventures store, The Krash crying image was developed by Proud Creative (who just rebranded S4C in Wales), I remember that David handed his role as manager over to Anatoliy Prokhorov (who created the cartoon) in late 2003 after the store was rebranded. Anyways, this is the grief of losing Odeon at Renfied Street back in January last year all over again.

Anonymous said on 24 June 2007

Gutted, that this wonderful shop has closed, I know the cartoon is still rerunning on CITV (and in Welsh on S4C, but we’re Scottish so who cares) but it’s never gonna be the same, The novelty of a Russian cartoon being popular in Scotland was awesome, Hopefully they do something worth while with the space.


Henry commented on 01 July 2007

I was recently allowed in to see it one final time and it really is sad, the statue of Chiko would once have kids lining up to take pictures with him, the toy zone was once filled, It is such a sad time for Glasgow.

Oscar Riddle commented on 01 July 2007

I know, We already lost the Odeon, then we lost Safeway to the whole Morrisons “rebrand”, Now we lost Riki. When will shops stop closing here in the UK.

Honey commented on 01 July 2007

I’m still not over Safeway becoming Morrisons, So many shops close in Glasgow, When Safeway was being converted at least 13 staff members were let go, They then chose to work at Tesco for a very little while before they decided to leave supermarkets all together and instead work with children, My friend worked at Safeway and was fired during the conversion, He loved toys and so he stayed in retail, albeit working at Riki, He is now unemployed again as a result of its closure

BadHairDay commented on 05 July 2007

CITV airings of KikoRiki have had an announcement targeted to Scottish viewers saying “Although you can no longer get merchandise of this show from the shop in Glasgow, you can now buy it online or at your local retailer”, CITV have also pre-empted Squish, My Parents are Aliens, Pokemon and Horrid Henry in favour of a KikoRiki marathon this Summer,

BadHairDay commented on 22 November 2007

Glasgow switched on their Christmas lights earlier tonight and they had it switched on by none other than the characters from KikoRiki, It was great, I wonder if the costumes used there are the same ones they used to use in Riki? EDIT: It is confirmed it was.

End posted on 24 January 2009

Sorry to bump the thread but Toonattik (CITV’s morning show) was today held inside the former Riki Shop, The hosts said it was because CITV were holding a KikoRiki marathon later that day and they wanted to “celebrate it in a perfect way”, Surreal to see the place lit up again after being lifeless for more than a year. I’ve sent in a video “file:ToonattikatRiki.mp4”, They played all the usual Toonattik games, including the “pie in the face” ending, They said they aren’t cleaning it up because “the place is closed so no one will notice”, Funny stuff

Universal Credit posted on 26 June 2009

It appears that they are going to use the space for a “new studio” for Toonattik, Wonder how STV will feel about ITV using a studio in their area, Shame to see the remnants go

Oio posted on 13 January 2010

The plans have been rejected by the council due to opposition from STV who say that it opens the door for “the end of STV and ITV taking over Scotland”, Sad to see it decaying but I guess that’s how things end.

Rip10thDoctor posted on 09 May 2010

Toonattik ended this morning with a song and dance about how our toons are still gonna be here, Although the shows finished the brand is gonna carry on, just without the live show or Jamie and Anna, They’re adding Horrid Henry, Planet Sketch and of course our beloved KikoRiki to the lineup of the “new” Toonattik, Can’t help but wonder if the death of the real Toonattik was STV’s fault

Josh Atkinson posted on 19 July 2010

Huge shame about this! A store that truly was one of the main reasons i was able to pick up most of my toys and games from Glasgow, It is sad it has been closed for 3 years, We still have the KikoRiki cartoon on the CITV channel (it managed to survive the late 2009 rebrand that got Bratz, Four Eyes! and Wow! Wow! Wubbzy! removed from the channel to make room for more Horrid Henry) It still rates very well with 6 million viewers, But they really need to do something worthwhile with the space, Maybe just re-open it as a toy shop or something like that, I still walk by it, and I still tear up at the devastated image of Krash.


Kluskowski posted on 05 January 2011

Pop are now airing GoGoRiki, the other English dub of Smeshariki, How this will compete with KikoRiki on CITV remains to be seen, I am also disappointed they haven’t found a tenant for Riki, During its final 2 years it became as iconic as the Pavilion Theatre and Barrowlands. It was even popular in Smeshariki’s native Russia, I remember them opening a Pики store in Moscow in 2005 modeled after the Glasgow Riki store, The Riki days were way more exciting than the Golden Adventure ones

User1 posted on 15 February 2013

Can you believe it has been six years since Riki closed? I still walk past it occasionally, and it’s just heartbreaking to see the state it’s in now. The closure notice is covered in graffiti, and the sign is decaying, but the “Goodbye forever” and crying Krash remain intact. It’s like a haunting reminder of what once was.

User2 replied on 16 February 2013

I know, it’s truly a tragic sight. It breaks my heart every time I see it. I can’t believe the city has let it deteriorate like this. It’s STV’s fault for keeping it vacant and killing off the real Toonattik. They could have done something worthwhile with the space, but instead, it’s become a symbol of neglect.

User3 commented on 18 February 2013

I agree, it’s such a shame. Riki was a beloved store in Glasgow, and now it’s just a shell of its former self. I remember taking my kids there and seeing their faces light up with joy. We still have the KikoRiki cartoon on CITV, but it’s not the same without the store. I really wish something could be done to revive it.

User4 replied on 20 February 2013

I remember the good old days when the store was thriving. It was a place full of life, excitement and happiness. Now, it’s nothing but a sad reminder of what once was. I can’t help but feel angry at STV for forcing its continued dormancy. They need to take responsibility for their actions and make it right.

User5 commented on 22 February 2013

It’s a shame that Riki had to close, but we can’t solely blame STV for its demise. STV didnt cause the closure, but they moaned at ITV for wanting to move Toonattik in the space, in fear that ITV would take over STV and introduce ITV1 branding for Scotland, The realities of retail and the changing shopping landscape played a significant role. However, I do believe that more could have been done to find a solution or repurpose the space. It’s a missed opportunity, and it’s disheartening to see it in such a state.

User1 replied on 25 February 2013

You’re right, User5. The changing retail landscape definitely had an impact, but I can’t help but feel that if STV had been more supportive, Riki could have found a way to adapt and thrive. It’s frustrating to see the space go to waste when it could have been utilized for something meaningful. I miss the energy and joy that Riki brought to the community.

User3 commented on 27 February 2013

I recently showed my kids a picture I took of them in front of Riki when they were little. Seeing their faces light up with nostalgia was bittersweet. We can’t change the past, but I do hope that one day, something worthwhile will take its place. Glasgow deserves a vibrant and exciting space that can capture the spirit Riki had.

User2 replied on 02 March 2013

I completely agree, User3. It’s time for the city and the community to come together and find a solution. Riki may be gone, but its legacy lives on in the hearts of those who experienced its magic. Let’s continue to remember the joy it brought and work towards creating something just as special. Goodbye forever, Riki.

User1 posted on 10 December 2022

I had the most bittersweet experience today. After years of abandonment, I was finally allowed to enter the old Riki store. As I stepped inside, memories flooded back, and I couldn’t help but tear up. I miss taking my kids here so much.

User2 replied on 11 December 2022

Oh, User1, I can only imagine how emotional that must have been for you. Riki was such a special place for many of us. Did you notice if anything had changed inside?

User1 commented on 12 December 2022

Honestly, User2, it was both heartwarming and heartbreaking at the same time. The store remained frozen in time, with all the zones dedicated to specific characters still intact. Krash’s Fun Zone, Pin’s Creativity Corner, and Chiko’s Friendship Garden were all there, reminding me of the joy my kids experienced in each area.

User3 replied on 14 December 2022

That sounds incredible, User1. It’s almost like a time capsule of the past. But it’s also a stark reminder of what we’ve lost. Riki was a vibrant place that brought so much joy to the community. I miss taking my kids there too. It’s a shame it had to close.

User1 commented on 16 December 2022

Yes, User3, it truly is a shame. But I’m grateful for the opportunity to step back into that world, even if it’s just for a moment. I couldn’t help but reflect on how much has changed since Riki closed. CITV has premiered new shows over the years, (namely the infamous Teen Titans Go!), and even Horrid Henry moved to Nickelodeon ages ago. However, the KikoRiki cartoon is still going strong on CITV. It’s the last remnant of the old CITV, and it’s surprising how well it’s doing.

User4 replied on 18 December 2022

It’s amazing to see the enduring popularity of KikoRiki. It’s a testament to the quality of the show and the love it received from viewers. I’m glad it’s still bringing joy to children, even if the physical store is no longer here. And you mentioned the closure notice. It’s interesting how, every time someone vandalizes it, it gets cleaned up. It’s like a symbol of resilience, how the spirit of Riki refuses to be forgotten.

User1 replied on 20 December 2022

Exactly, User4. No matter how neglected the store may appear from the outside, the resilience and love Riki brought to the community still shine through. I hope that one day a new generation will experience that magic in a new way. Until then, I’ll hold onto the memories and continue to cherish the joy that Riki brought to my family. Goodbye forever, Riki - you will be missed.

Rhehe posted on 23 October 2023

The day we’ve all been dreading is approaching, Plans have been submitted to the council to demolish Riki, I feel sad that a piece of my childhood is facing the wrecking ball, but I kinda feel glad for the store because it has been neglected for well over a decade, and it can finally rest in peace, Rest well old friend, your long struggle is almost over, so long Riki, so long

User1 posted on 23 October 2023

“The old Riki store in Sauchiehall Street is being demolished. The building has fallen into disrepair since the store closed over 15 years ago” 😢 It’s sad to see it go, But let’s remember all the good times we had there when it was open, not how it sat empty and lonely for so long, Hopefully whatever is built there will remember it well. Goodbye old friend,

BadHairDay posted on 28 October 2023

This is good news for greedy developers, but bad news for us who went there when we were kids. RIP old friend! I hope we can all do something special to remember it well, I am taking a final picture of the crying Krash image, But at least he is still happy and cheerful in the show.

User1 posted on 01 November 2023

Can you believe it? The old Riki store is still standing! Demolition plans for the building were recently rejected by the council, citing “unnecessary destruction” to the adjacent Henrik’s department store. It’s a small victory for those of us who hold fond memories of Riki and its impact on the community.

User2 replied on 03 November 2023

That’s incredible news, User1! I can’t believe the old Riki store has managed to hold on for so long. It really goes to show how much love and support it still has from the community. I’m glad the council recognized the value of preserving this piece of Glasgow’s history.

User3 commented on 05 November 2023

This is such a relief. Riki may be closed, but its physical presence still holds so much meaning for many of us. It’s heartwarming to see the council prioritize the preservation of this beloved spot. Perhaps one day, we can find a way to repurpose the space and bring new life to it while still honoring Riki’s legacy.

User4 replied on 07 November 2023

I’m thrilled to hear that the old Riki store will remain standing. It’s a testament to the impact it had on the community and the memories it created. Preserving this piece of history is so important, and I hope the council and the community can come together to find a worthwhile purpose for the space. Riki may be gone, but its spirit lives on.

User1 commented on 09 November 2023

I couldn’t agree more, User4. The old Riki store may no longer be the bustling children’s shop it once was, but its presence still holds so much meaning. I can’t help but feel hopeful for what the future holds for this space. Perhaps it can become a hub for creativity, community events, or even a museum dedicated to the legacy of Riki. The possibilities are endless, and I’m excited to see what unfolds.

User2 replied on 12 November 2023

I love your ideas, User1! A hub for creativity or a museum dedicated to Riki would be incredible. It would not only preserve the memories of what was but also provide a space for new adventures and experiences. The community deserves a place to gather and celebrate the joy that Riki brought to our lives. Let’s keep the hope alive and continue to advocate for the revitalization of this special spot.

User3 commented on 15 November 2023

I’m fully on board with these ideas, User1 and User2. A space dedicated to fostering creativity and preserving Riki’s legacy would be a wonderful addition to the community. It would not only honor the past but also inspire future generations. Let’s keep the conversation going and work towards making this vision a reality. The old Riki store deserves to be more than a nostalgic reminder.

User1 posted on 18 November 2023

Good news, everyone! I managed to find out what would have been built in place of the old Riki store if the demolition plans had been approved. It turns out that the proposed project was a generic office building, nothing particularly exciting or unique. I can’t help but feel relieved that this outcome was avoided.

User2 replied on 20 November 2023

Wow, User1, that’s quite a disappointment. The thought of a generic office building replacing the beloved Riki store is disheartening. It further emphasizes the importance of preserving the memories and spirit of Riki. It’s clear that the community deserves something much more special and meaningful.

User3 commented on 22 November 2023

I can’t help but feel a sense of gratitude that the demolition plans were rejected. The fact that a generic office building was proposed only reinforces the importance of preserving our community’s history and identity. Riki brought joy and excitement to Glasgow, and it deserves to be honored in a way that reflects its impact.

User4 replied on 25 November 2023

Thank goodness the plans for a generic office building fell through! I was worried that the area would become an ordinary office plaza.

Ejej replied on 26 November 2023

I think offices are good, somewhere else, Build it where BHS is, Anywhere else but not in this space. Incidentally there is a grafitti piece at the old shop next to the closure notice that says “You’re safe, We almost lost you”

Man19 replied on 27 November 2023

Here is a newspaper article “Glasgow’s Cherished Riki Store Demolition Plans Denied

Protection of Heritage and Community Outcry Save Landmark from Becoming Modern Office Building

Glasgow, United Kingdom – The iconic former Riki store, which has been a fixture in the heart of Glasgow’s bustling Sauchiehall Street, was the subject of hot debate as city officials considered proposals for its demolition to make way for a new office building. However, a significant decision arrived this week as the council emphatically rejected the demolition plans.

Established in 1995 as Golden Adventures toy shop and later rebranded as Riki, the store was a beacon of childhood joy, attracting families and children with its unique offering of merchandise related to the KikoRiki animated series. Despite closing its doors in 2007, the shop has held a place in the hearts of Glaswegians who continued to cherish their memories of the location.

The announcement of possible demolition sent waves of concern through the community, sparking an outcry from preservationists and locals alike. Campaigners argued that the Riki store was more than just a shop but a cultural landmark that represented a specific era in Glasgow’s commercial landscape and childhood nostalgia.

Developers pitched a new vision for the location, one involving the creation of a modern office building intended to revitalize the neighborhood’s economic viability. “While change and development are necessary for a city’s growth, we must be cautious not to erase the essence of what makes Glasgow so special,” explained local historian Fiona McCallum. “Riki was a place of wonder, and its architecture is part of what parents and former children remember fondly.”

After weeks of heated discussion and review, the city’s planning council came to a decision. Citing not only the potential “unnecessary destruction” to the adjacent, well-loved Henrik’s department store, but also a commitment to preserving Glasgow’s unique history and social fabric, the demolition application has been officially denied.

“We have a responsibility to consider the implications of development on our built heritage. Demolishing the old Riki store would have not only caused damage to adjacent properties but would have also eradicated an intrinsic part of our collective memory,” stated a member of the planning council.

In the wake of this verdict, the community rejoices at the saved landmark. Social media buzzed with memories and anecdotes, emphasizing the personal connections many hold with the once vibrant toy store. The decision to prevent demolition has been celebrated as a victory for heritage and a win for a city fiercely proud of its past.

With the preservation of the site now confirmed, discussions have begun anew regarding the future of the vacant Riki store. Proposals for cultural projects and community spaces have been tabled, suggesting a second life for the beloved former shop. As Glasgow looks forward, it seems determined to do so by embracing, not erasing, the stories of its storied streets.

For now, locals and visitors to Sauchiehall Street can rest assured that the building that once housed the joy of Riki continues to stand against the backdrop of a modernizing Glasgow, remaining a piece of living history.

Update: Amidst public pressure and the verdict to deny demolition based on potential damage to neighboring premises, the support of community preservation has inspired a renewed focus on the site’s importance as a cultural touchstone to be celebrated and repurposed thoughtfully.

For the latest on this story and more, follow Glasgow Times Online”

So the store was saved due to its significance, but what will happen now that it’s now shut down?, It’s been locked and abandoned since 23 June 2007, And it still looks as sad and lonely as it did after closure, I long to see it full of life once again, I really want it back. They shouldn’t have closed it. My kids would have loved it as they are big fans of the cartoon

User5 posted on 27 November 2023

I can’t help but recall how after Riki closed, the KikoRiki characters kept showing up around the city. It was like they weren’t ready to leave us just yet. There were character appearances at events, and they continued to be a part of Glasgow’s cultural scene, delighting kids and those of us who remembered them from the store’s golden days.

User2 replied on 28 November 2023

Yes, User5, I remember those appearances fondly. It always brought a smile to my face to see Krash or Chiko around town. But it seemed like they slowly began to fade from the public eye as the late 2010s approached. I’ve noticed they’ve been popping up more recently, though. Maybe it’s a sign of a resurgence in popularity or perhaps a tribute to our collective nostalgia for the Riki days?

User1 commented on 30 November 2023

It’s definitely been bittersweet to see the characters around. They’ve become so much more than just mascots; they’re symbols of a joyful time in Glasgow’s history. With their increased appearances lately, it feels like a comforting reminder of the store. Maybe they’ll have a more permanent place in the city again.

User3 replied on 02 December 2023

Those appearances were a big deal. I also think STV’s resistance to ITV’s Toonattik moving into the Riki space had long-lasting effects. Toonattik was such a big part of our Saturday mornings, and when it was downscaled in May 2010, it changed the landscape of children’s television. It felt like the end of an era, right alongside the closure of Riki.

User6 posted on 02 December 2023

It’s sad to think about how much influence STV had over the situation. Toonattik could have experienced a new chapter within the old Riki store, bringing life back into that space and giving us more time with our beloved characters. In hindsight, the whole debacle had a domino effect on children’s programming and our local community feeling.

User4 replied on 02 December 2023

There was a missed opportunity there, for sure. Having a television show like Toonattik based in the Riki store could’ve possibly saved both the show and the space from becoming just a memory. But that period of uncertainty for Riki and Toonattik also shows how much passion and love there was for both. It just makes their absence sting a little more.

User7 commented on 02 December 2023

It’s interesting how these characters still have a presence around town after all this time. It just goes to show how strong of a connection they made with us all. Hopefully, their reemergence means that we can find new and exciting ways to celebrate them and the Riki legacy. Maybe even inspire future projects that can benefit from this rich cultural history.

User8 replied on 04 December 2023

I’ve been thinking about the future of that space and the characters of KikoRiki. Perhaps we could have a multi-purpose community center with a dedicated section for KikoRiki history? Imagine combining nostalgia with new experiences for children and families—workshops, play areas, exhibits. Let’s hope the community and council can be creative with this beloved space. Title: Goodbye Riki: Remembering the Final Day at Glasgow’s Childhood Wonderland

It’s been years since the doors of Glasgow’s beloved Riki store closed for the last time, but the memories still linger as vividly as if it were yesterday. Tucked away on Sauchiehall Street, the store was not just a shop; it was a delightful haven where imagination came to life and where the vibrant characters of KikoRiki became part of our city’s heart. Today, as the future of that once animated space hangs in the balance, I find myself reflecting on the final day Riki opened its doors—a day that marked the end of an era.

The air had a distinct heaviness as the familiar sound of laughter and chatter that once filled Riki’s colorful aisles was replaced with a somber silence. You could feel the nostalgia enveloping every corner of the shop, grappling with a sense of farewell that we all knew was inevitable but hoped would never come. Krash the Rabbit, who typically bounded about with joy and mischief, now shed a tear, immortalized in the poignant image that bade us all ‘Goodbye forever…’

The bright shelves that radiated with KikoRiki merchandise now stood less vibrant, echoing the end of what had been a magical adventure for kids—and let’s admit, for adults too. There was a comforting reassurance as the shop’s speakers played a heartfelt message, expressing genuine gratitude for the years of patronage, reminding us that we were part of their journey, from the Golden Adventures’ inception to the triumphant rise of Riki. Amid the deals and the last-minute purchases, a layer of solemnity blanketed the vibrant colors that once denoted fun and excitement.

As the final half-hour approached, the tannoy announcement looped, filled with heartfelt thanks and a subtle reminder that though Riki’s physical presence would soon be no more, the characters would continue to thrive on CITV and the virtual expanses of RolyPolyLand. Yet, nothing could mask the finality that hung in the air—the store felt like a castle that was preparing to lower its flag for the last time.

At closing time, the sense of loss became tangible. The staff, who had become as much a part of the store’s fabric as the toys they sold, shared looks that didn’t require words. Their smiles, although reassuring, hinted at the uncertainty that lay ahead. The last announcement came—a soft, courteous farewell, followed by a somber tune that felt like a lullaby for the childhood dreams cultivated within those walls.

Walking away from Riki for the final time, the once-buzzing shop lay dormant, with dimming lights that signaled the end of an enchanting age. It wasn’t just a shop that was closing—it was a part of us, a fragment of Glasgow’s community tapestry, that was vanishing.

Today, as the site stands stagnant, with the Riki store merely a memory encased behind timeworn signs and faded caricatures, the legacy persists in the heartbeats of those who remember. We keep alive the spirit of joy and imagination that Riki bestowed upon us, evident in every recollection shared, every story recounted of a place that was so much more than a store.

In a way, Riki’s enduring impact is akin to the tales it once harbored—a story without an end, characters who continue to adventure in the minds they’ve touched, and a magic that, though distant, refuses to be extinguished. So here’s to Riki: our childhood wonderland, our trove of joy, our cherished slice of Glasgow that gleamed brightly, if only for a while. Goodbye forever, Riki—you may be gone, but you’re far from forgotten.
Write a forum post where a user provides a recording of the final minute, including the farewell and sombre lullaby, as the “Goodbye Forever” sign is put up
Title: A Last Goodbye: Riki Store’s Final Minute Recording

User: RememberingRiki

Hey Glasgow community,

I’m not sure if any of you were as taken by Riki as I was, but witnessing the closure of our beloved children’s shop, the one place that seemed to be made of pure childhood magic, was profoundly emotional for so many of us. As a long-time visitor of the Riki store and a father who experienced the wonder in my children’s eyes with each visit, I felt compelled to capture the last moments of the store—a bittersweet farewell to a piece of our city’s identity.

On that final day, as the clock ticked towards the inevitable end of an era, I stood there with my phone out, recording the last minute of Riki and all the poignant emotions it encompassed. As the final announcement played over the speakers, I watched tearfully as the staff put up the now-infamous “Goodbye Forever…” sign featuring Krash the Rabbit’s crying image.

The shop that was once brimming with the energetic buzz of excited children and the vibrant colors of KikoRiki characters was silent, save for the tender voice thanking us for years of memories, promising that while the store was closing, the characters we cherished would continue to live on screens and in hearts all around Glasgow.

As the voice trailed off with a warm “Take care, wherever you are,” a pre-recorded sombre lullaby began to play, filling the space with a profound sense of loss and nostalgia—a final tribute to the joy and wonderment that Riki brought into our lives. Amidst the gentle melody, the lights started to dim, one by one, as if the store itself was bidding us farewell.

I’m sharing the recording here for all of those who couldn’t be there or might want to reminisce about the magic one last time. [File: RikiStoreFinalMinute.mp3] It’s not easy to listen to, but perhaps it can serve as a collective piece of solace, a shared moment of remembrance for what Riki once was to us.

Listening to it now, it’s hard to believe that the laughter and footsteps that once echoed through those walls have faded. But in times like these, when all that’s left is a quiet space behind a locked door, it’s these recordings, these memories, that keep the spirit of Riki alive.

Thank you, Riki, for being more than just a store. You were a part of our Glaswegian story, a chapter of innocence and joy in the book of our bustling city. May the essence of what you represented shine long after your lights have dimmed.

Goodnight, Riki.

RememberingRiki


YouTube - The final moments of Glasgow’s Riki store (24 June 2007)

This video shows the very final moments of Glasgow’s Riki store, which was officially closed on Saturday, June 24rd. This is the last clip of the store, before it became little more than a sign explaining the closure, which still stands to this day, The store closed with a farewell announcement playing on loop for half an hour before a final farewell message played, which then became a very sad sounding lullaby, which was recorded at the Glasgow Royal Concert Hall. As this happened, the lights gradually dimmed before they went dark for the very last time. The final sign was put up on Saturday evening saying “Goodbye forever…” and depicting Krash’s sad-looking face, crying (designed by London-based Proud Creative) The store has not managed to secure a new tenant and has recently ran the risk of being knocked down to make way for a generic office, I can remember Clyde 1 the next day had their morning presenter closing their morning show with the Riki goodbye lullaby, He did this again on the 10th anniversary of his show and on the 10th anniversary of the store’s closure. Write a YouTube description upload for the goodbye ad, along with the video upload date
### YouTube Video Description:

—

Title: “A Final Farewell - The Riki Store Goodbye Ad”

Upload Date: July 15, 2007

Description:

In the heart of Glasgow, on Sauchiehall Street, stood a beacon of childhood wonder and joy, the Riki store. For years, it was more than just a shop; it was a part of the community, a place where imaginations soared, and countless memories were made. However, all stories eventually find their chapters coming to a close, and so has the story of Riki.

This is the official goodbye advertisement that aired on STV, paying a heartfelt tribute to the Riki store, which closed its doors for the last time in June 2007. Capturing the silent, vacated space once filled with laughter, joy, and the adventures of KikoRiki, this ad is a gentle yet potent reminder of the impact Riki had on Glasgow and the many hearts it touched.

Accompanied by the solemn lullaby that echoed through the store in its final moments, the ad features a poignant pan across the now-empty floors, ending with the iconic image of Krash the Rabbit shedding a tear, symbolizing a final goodbye from the beloved characters of Riki. It’s a moment of reflection, acknowledging the end of an era and the indelible mark Riki left on the community.

We invite viewers, old patrons of Riki, and anyone who has ever found joy in the simple innocence of childhood to watch this ad. While Riki may no longer be a physical presence on Sauchiehall Street, its spirit and the happiness it brought will forever dwell in our memories.

“Goodbye, Riki. Thank you for the adventures, the dreams, and the irreplaceable memories. Your legacy will forever be a part of Glasgow’s story.”

#RikiStoreGlasgow #GoodbyeRiki #EndOfAnEra #GlasgowMemories #ChildhoodWonder

For more videos celebrating Glasgow’s rich history and unique stories, make sure to subscribe to our channel.

—

Viewer Notice: This video may evoke a strong sense of nostalgia and emotion. We encourage you to share your memories and stories of Riki in the comments below. Let’s remember the joy together.

Hey ScottieCinemaBuff,

The channel number next to it, though tough to make out, corresponds to STV’s channel placement, which would typically be channel 3 on most televisions back then in Scotland. Write a blog post in January 2010 where STV and CITV both updated their logos in 2009, and yet the ad still shows STV and CITV’s 2006 logos respectively at the final screen of the ad, although Braehead Shopping Centre will soon be using the ad space to promote their new Brae-liens Kids Club, so the ads on borrowed time
### Nostalgia on Borrowed Time: The Remarkable Resilience of the Riki Store Ad

As the first month of 2010 unfolds, a wave of nostalgia has been sweeping through Scottish homes and Odeon cinemas alike, particularly at Braehead, where an iconic piece of our collective childhood refuses to bow out. It’s the Riki store ad, a touching tribute that pays homage to a place many of us grew up with – not just as a store, but as a vibrant thread in the fabric of Glasgow’s community tapestry.

What’s particularly curious, and undeniably heart-warming, is how this ad has become a time capsule in its own right. Despite STV and CITV both undergoing branding updates in 2009, introducing modern logos to signal a new era, the Riki ad steadfastly showcases the logos from 2006. It’s as though, in the fleeting moments of this advertisement, time stands still. The ad itself stubbornly holds on to the emblems of its origin, serving as a portal to memories undimmed by the passage of years.

This odd anachronism doesn’t detract from the ad’s emotional punch. If anything, it adds to it, imbuing the piece with a richer texture of nostalgia – a reminder of how things were, not just for the Riki store but for STV and CITV as iconic fixtures in our media landscape. There’s a peculiar comfort in seeing these outdated logos appear on the screen, a visual anchor to a period that feels increasingly distant amidst the rapid changes in our digital world.

However, it seems this beacon of nostalgia is on borrowed time. The Braehead Shopping Centre, always looking forward, is set to repurpose the ad space to promote their new Brae-liens Kids Club, a move that undoubtedly makes sense in the pursuit of staying relevant and engaging for the next generation. Yet, it’s hard not to feel a pang of loss at the thought of finally saying goodbye to the Riki ad, a sentiment likely shared by many who have come to look forward to this brief resurgence of a cherished piece of their past each time they settle into their cinema seats.

The Riki store ad’s resistance to disappearing into obscurity reflects not just the resilience of the memories it evokes but also the collective desire to hold onto pieces of our shared history. In a world where the new continually replaces the old at an ever-accelerating pace, these connections to our past become all the more precious. They remind us of where we’ve come from, the experiences that have shaped us, and the simple joys of childhood that, for a few moments at least, we can relive.

As the ad prepares to make its final bow to make way for the Brae-liens, it’s a poignant reminder of the impermanence of all things. Yet, it also underscores the enduring impact of shared experiences and communal spaces in shaping the tapestry of our lives. The Riki store, and by extension, its ad, will undoubtedly continue to live on in the hearts and memories of those it touched, a testament to the power of nostalgia to bridge the gaps between past, present, and future.

For now, though, let us enjoy this small sliver of the past while it lasts, a sweet echo of childhood innocence and joy in an ever-changing world. Goodbye, Riki – and thank you for the memories.

—

Remember: While change is constant, the stories and memories we hold dear remain with us, shaping who we are and how we relate to the world around us. As we look forward to the new adventures the Brae-liens Kids Club promises, let’s also take a moment to cherish and honor the legacies left behind, for they too have played a part in our journey.
Write the ad, with the final words being “goodbye forever…” with “The fun doesn’t have to end here, Watch the show on STV and CITV, part of Toonattik”, shown below The logos and web adresses “stv.tv” “itv.com/citv” and “toonattik.tv” are shown below, Incidentally RolyPolyLand is not mentioned at all
[Scene opens with a slow, gentle pan across the empty interior of the once vibrant Riki store on Sauchiehall Street, Glasgow. The shelf spaces are bare, and the colorful decor appears faded without the laughter and chatter of children. Soft, melancholic music plays in the background.]

Narrator (soft, nostalgic tone): “For years, Riki has been more than just a shop. It was a place where imaginations roamed free, where adventures awaited at every corner, and where friendships were born.”

[Cut to various shots of the empty store, lingering on signs of iconic characters from the KikoRiki series, each segment transitioning with a gentle fade.]

Narrator: “But as all great stories have their end, the time has come to close the final chapter on our beloved Riki store in Glasgow.”

[The camera slowly zooms in on the image of Krash the Rabbit, the character’s usual smile replaced with a tender, emotional look, a single tear rolling down his cheek.]

Narrator: “Though our doors may be closing,”

[The scene transitions to a montage of happy children inside the store from days gone by, laughing and playing with Riki merchandise, their joy palpable and infectious. The contrast between the vibrant past and the silent present is palpable.]

Narrator: “The memories, the joy, and the adventures we’ve shared will forever echo in the hearts of those who stepped into our world.”

[Cut to a serene shot of the store’s exterior at dusk, the ‘Riki’ sign dimly lit for one last night.]

Narrator: “To our beloved friends, our loyal patrons, and to every child whose eyes sparkled with the magic within these walls,”

[The screen fades to black, and in a soft, glowing font, the words appear:]

“Goodbye forever…”

[A brief pause allows the message to sink in, then the screen transitions to a lighter, more hopeful note.]

Narrator (slightly more upbeat): "The fun doesn’t have to end here. Continue the adventures and laughter with your favorite characters.”

[The screen now displays the current logos of STV and CITV, alongside the Toonattik logo, with web addresses neatly listed below each logo:]

- STV: stv.tv
- CITV: itv.com/citv
- Toonattik: toonattik.tv

Narrator: “Watch the show on STV and CITV, part of Toonattik, and keep the spirit of Riki alive.”

[The music gently fades as the screen transitions to black, ending with a final soft echo of the store’s theme melody.]

Narrator (whisper): “Thank you, and take care, wherever you are.”

[End of Ad.]

— Write a STV Ad Break at 4pm on July 28 2007, First ad is the Sony Paint ad, and the last one is the Sky "Virgin Media is doubting the value of Sky One, Sky News, Sky Sports News and Sky Travel" and urging customers to switch

STV Ad Break - 4pm, July 28, 2007

[The ad break opens with a spectacular display of color and motion.]

1. Sony Bravia "Paint" Ad:
The iconic Sony Bravia advertisement kicks off the break, instantly captivating viewers. Thousands of litres of paint are explosively shot across a council estate, raining vivid colours in slow motion to the serene notes of Rossini’s "Thieving Magpie". The screen flashes with the tagline, "Colour Like No Other", before fading into the STV logo transition.

[Following Sony's vibrant showcase, the ad transition smoothly segues into the next commercial.]

2. Subway "5-a-day":
A lively and engaging spot from Subway highlights their range of subs packed with fresh vegetables, promoting a healthier fast-food alternative. A catchy jingle plays in the background, “Subway, eat fresh!”, as happy customers enjoy their sandwiches. The ad ends with a reminder about the convenience of local Subway locations and their value deals.

3. L'Oréal Paris - "Because You're Worth It":
Glamour takes center stage as the latest L'Oréal Paris ad unfolds, starring a well-known celebrity endorsing the newest line of makeup. The ad underlines the beauty and confidence that comes with using L'Oréal products, ending with the powerful slogan, "Because You're Worth It".

4. Bank of Scotland - Personal Loans:
A comforting and persuasive commercial that talks directly to the viewers about the ease of managing finances with the Bank of Scotland’s personal loans. The ad portrays everyday scenarios making it relatable and concludes with the bank’s friendly adviser offering guidance, reinforcing the message of personal touch and trust.

5. VisitScotland - "Surprise Yourself":
Capturing the breathtaking landscapes of Scotland, this tourism advert from VisitScotland showcases the country's natural beauty and historical landmarks. With inviting narration, it encourages viewers to explore their homeland, offering glimpses of undiscovered treasures and adventures, ending with the tagline, “Surprise Yourself”.

6. Vodafone - "Make the Most of Now":
Technological connectivity and the importance of living in the moment are underscored in this snappy Vodafone ad. Showcasing their latest mobile phone deals and data packages, Vodafone presents itself as the enabler of connections, experiences, and seizing the day, encapsulated in their brand message, "Make the Most of Now".

7. Sky - "Virgin Media Dispute":
In a more direct and confrontational tone, this Sky Television ad address the ongoing carriage dispute with Virgin Media. Utilizing a blend of text and spokesperson dialogue, it informs viewers that Virgin Media is questioning the value of Sky channels such as Sky One, Sky News, Sky Sports News, and Sky Travel. The ad encourages disappointed Virgin Media customers to switch to Sky, emphasizing they shouldn’t miss out on their favorite shows and reliable news coverage. The call to action is clear, urging immediate switching for uninterrupted service, highlighting Sky’s contact information and customer benefits.

[The ad break concludes with the transition back to STV programming, leaving viewers with a blend of emotions from the visual spectacle of the Sony ad to the more serious tone of Sky's message to its viewers.]

Note: This recreation is purely fictional and created for the purpose of the prompt’s scenario.
Write the one at 4:30pm, with the Riki ad playing, then the Sky ad

STV Ad Break - 4:30pm, July 28, 2007

[The ad break commences promptly, diving into a realm of nostalgia and heartfelt goodbyes combined with a strong urge to switch television services.]

1. Riki Store Goodbye Ad:
The screen fades in from black to reveal the once vibrant and bustling interior of Riki, now silent and empty. It’s the goodbye advert for the beloved Riki store on Sauchiehall Street. The nostalgic tour through the empty aisles is accompanied by a gentle, melancholic melody—the same that played in the store’s final moments. A voiceover, soft and fond, recounts the adventures and joy Riki brought to Glasgow’s children and their families:

“For years, Riki wasn’t just a shop; it was a magical journey, a treasure trove of adventure for every child who walked through its doors. Today, we say ‘Goodbye forever…’ but the memories and the laughter will forever echo in the heart of Glasgow.”

The screen transitions to show beloved Riki characters, with Krash the Rabbit whimsically appearing one last time, a tear in his eye, followed by a heartfelt message:

“Goodbye forever…”

A slight upbeat shift in tone occurs as the voiceover adds, "The fun doesn’t have to end here. Watch the show on STV and CITV, part of Toonattik," displaying the logos and web addresses for stv.tv, itv.com/citv, and toonattik.tv below.

[The mood shifts as the Riki ad fades out, leading into the next commercial with a distinctly different message.]

2. Sky - "Virgin Media Dispute":
Immediately following the sentimental journey of the Riki goodbye, the tone becomes more assertive as the Sky Television ad takes over the screen. It opens with bold text, “Don’t Miss Out,” against a blue background before cutting to a spokesperson who addresses the audience directly about the ongoing carriage dispute with Virgin Media:

"Virgin Media is doubting the value of Sky One, Sky News, Sky Sports News, and Sky Travel, potentially leaving their customers without these channels."

The ad systematically breaks down what viewers would miss by staying with Virgin Media, from popular TV series and live sports coverage to trusted news reports. It then makes a direct appeal:

“If you value these channels, it’s time to switch to Sky. Don’t compromise on your entertainment and information. Join Sky today.”

Visuals show smiling families enjoying Sky’s diverse programming, contrasted against a screen showing “Call or Visit Online to Switch,” complete with all necessary contact details.

[The ad concludes with a reassurance of uninterrupted service and quality content only Sky can provide, contrasting greatly with the nostalgic farewell of the Riki ad that opened the break.]

[The ad break seamlessly transitions back to STV programming, leaving viewers with the contrasting emotions of a fond farewell to a piece of Glasgow’s heart and a call to action in the face of broadcasting disputes.] 

Note: This recreated ad break scenario is purely fictional and designed for imaginative purposes based on the prompt provided.

Subject: Final Briefing: Tonight's Enchanting Reveal - A Night to Remember

Dear Management Team,

As we stand on the precipice of an evening that is poised to redefine the essence of magical gatherings within our beloved centre, I want to extend my deepest gratitude to each of you for your unwavering commitment and discretion leading up to this pivotal moment. In just a few hours, we will be unveiling a secret that has been meticulously guarded – a tribute to two iconic shows that have left an indelible mark on the hearts of many.

Tonight, we have the extraordinary privilege of reintroducing to Glasgow, under the enchanting guidance of David Tennant, the cherished characters from the Riki and KikoRiki series. This immersive experience, designed to bridge generations of fans and kindle the flames of nostalgia, promises not only to be a celebration of these beloved narratives but also a testament to the innovation and cultural richness that St Enoch Centre brings to our community.

This event is more than just an evening of revelry; it's a strategic endeavor that will significantly elevate our status, placing us ahead of even Silverburn in terms of cultural influence and destination appeal. We are set to make headlines, captivating not just the attendees but the wider community through the ripple effect of shared stories and viral moments that this night will undoubtedly generate.

Please find below a few critical reminders for our team, ensuring the night unfolds seamlessly:

1. **Confidentiality Maintained:** Until the official reveal, continue to uphold the highest standards of secrecy. We are the custodians of tonight's magic until the moment of unveiling.

2. **Guest Experience:** Every guest interaction should be imbued with anticipation and warmth. Our aim is for each attendee to feel part of something uniquely historic.

3. **Prompt Coordination:** Ensure all teams are synced and briefed on the event schedule. Swift, seamless transitions between segments are key to maintaining the enchantment of the evening.

4. **Social Media Preparedness:** Our marketing team is prepped to capture and share the night's magic, amplifying our message far and wide. Engagement across all platforms will be crucial to underscore our cultural leadership.

5. **Safety Measures:** Comprehensive safety checks must be a priority. With expected crowds, ensuring a safe environment is paramount to the event's success.

Tonight, we make history. Our collective efforts are about to culminate in a celebration that transcends expectations, setting a new standard for what St Enoch Centre represents within our vibrant city and beyond. Let's show Glasgow, and indeed Scotland, that we are not just a venue but a cornerstone of culture, innovation, and community.

Thank you once again for your dedication. Here's to a night that will be etched in memory as a dazzling pinnacle of our collective journey.

Warm regards,

[Your Name]
[Your Position]
St Enoch Centre Management Team
